lockStatus.notes: Civilian names and final casting details can lock at script without breaking structure.

organization.name: Lattice
organization.roleInSeason: Primary antagonist force — surveillance, recruitment pressure, direct action, and continuity research tied to Wraith's origin.

theArchitect.publicPresentation: Speaks like infrastructure: calm, inevitable, precise. Dresses controlled — nothing flashy unless it serves intimidation. Moves like someone who used to save people and still believes they are saving them.
theArchitect.originSketch: Former hero (details of old codename and public fall — **TBD at script**). Believes unregulated superhumans collapse civic trust faster than any villain. Lattice is their instrument — or they are Lattice's mind — Season 1 can keep the reporting structure ambiguous until late; what cannot waver is motivation and moral self-image.
theArchitect.motivation: Superhumans are too dangerous to exist freely and must be managed. They believe they are protecting humanity — not destroying it. Destruction is a bug; control is the product.
theArchitect.ideologicalTwist: They may be right enough to disturb — especially Elias (Solace), who recognizes sincerity faster than he recognizes malice. That makes them more dangerous than cackling evil: the story can argue back without strawmen.
methods: Framing choices as triage: who gets contained, who gets leveraged, who gets erased from the narrative.
Weaponizing psychology in Episodes 10–12 — fear profiles per Nova member (per `Season 1/season.json` Ep 10 brief).
Preferring institutional outcomes over personal grudges — personal cruelty can appear, but it should feel like policy with a face.
theArchitect.relationshipToLattice: Occupies or eclipses the **Principal** slot from the series bible hierarchy — whether they founded Lattice, inherited it, or hijack it in Season 1 is **draft** until writers' room locks continuity.
theArchitect.powersAndThreatLevel: **Draft:** Former hero implies combat competence and tactical genius. Avoid omniscience; their edge is preparation, narrative control, and resources — not invulnerability. Finale allows defeat without obliteration: **defeated but not destroyed** (Ep 12 brief) with grudging respect and a warning — they remain a future reckoning.
theArchitect.defeatOutcomeSeason1: Loss of immediate endgame (city-scale control objective — **specify in script**), survival with status damaged, threat dormant not erased. Nova Force wins publicly; the idea of management survives in the culture.
theArchitect.voiceNotes: No cartoon villain laugh. Warmth used as a tool. When challenged morally, slows down — listens too carefully — which reads as creepier than shouting.
theArchitect.visualHook: Architecture motifs: grids, blueprints-as-cloth, clean lines vs the team's messy humanity. Color palette cool and authoritative — contrast with hero warmth (see `series-bible.json` heroColorKeys).

figures.name: Rook
figures.visualHook: Binocular gloss and cheap coffee stains — someone who lives in vans and rooftops. Unremarkable clothes; memorable posture: too still.
figures.roleInPlot: Episodes 1–2: eyes on the pulse zone and early powered activity — the 'shadowy organization watching' from Ep 1; crosshair on Wraith/Surge friction in Ep 2.
figures.outcome: Does not fight to win publicly — extracts intel and vanishes. Survives; may recur as background threat.

figures.name: Agent Maren Vale
figures.visualHook: Tailored wrong for the neighborhood — too clean. Smile that costs money. Hands always visible — 'trust me' body language as weapon.
figures.roleInPlot: Episode 3: Titan public debut draws Lattice response — recruit or remove Remi before they become a symbol.
figures.outcome: Fails the clean recruit; fight or chase ends inconclusively enough that Remi survives viral — Vale retreats with a dossier thicker than before.

figures.name: Weaver
figures.visualHook: Never a body early — only UI: a calm glyph that rethreads Priya's hacks back at her. If embodied later, androgynous, too-perfect hair, wrong frame rate.
figures.roleInPlot: Episode 4: Glitch steals encrypted data; Weaver is the system's immune response — chase, counter-intrusion, panic as comedy turns sharp.
figures.outcome: Priya keeps partial data; Weaver locks her out hard enough to force real-world consequences — sets up Ep 6 strike path.

figures.name: Echo
figures.visualHook: Repeating motion tells — same step twice, laugh twice — subtle wrongness. Restraint harness under clothes; Lattice failsafe glow at neck or wrist.
figures.roleInPlot: Episode 5: villain drawn from Glitch's intel. Elias refuses the finishing blow; Echo escapes — proving mercy has a bill. Elias notices control, not cruelty.
figures.outcome: Escape (per brief). Sets moral template: some enemies are prisoners. May recur as tragedy, not moustache villain.

figures.name: Stray
figures.visualHook: Tactical gear with Lattice sigil abstracted to 'corporate sports team.' Helmet optional; eyes always read tired — true believer in procedure.
figures.roleInPlot: Episode 6: attacks Glitch's safehouse — forces first full team-up. Henchmen-heavy; Stray is the voice yelling orders through smoke.
figures.outcome: Tactical loss — team wins messy. Stray extracted by protocol; leaves behind evidence that Lattice has scale.

figures.name: Dr. Aris Thorne
figures.visualHook: Soft hands, hard watch. Lab coat as costume even in flashback. Smile that says 'consent' while signing harm.
figures.roleInPlot: Episode 7: backstory reveal — childhood experiment lattice (organization) links to Nadia's shadow curse. May appear in testimony, recording, or present-day probe — not always a fistfight antagonist.
figures.outcome: Emotional victory over ideological: Nadia stays, but Thorne's institution survives her rage — files exist, guilt exists. Survives to Season 2 potential; escape or protected by lawyers in-world.

figures.name: Marrow
figures.type: Tactical closer — mission that fractures Surge/Glitch trust
figures.visualHook: Brutalist speed — straight lines, elbow-first. Gear built to punish clustered heroes (nets, directed EMP-adjacent tech — **not magic**).
figures.roleInPlot: Episode 8: fallout mission from prior Lattice pressure — Marrow exploits Surge's impulse; Glitch ends up bruised enough to ask the painful question (per Ep 8 brief).
figures.outcome: Mission 'won' but trust damaged — Marrow's win condition is relational, not kill-count. Retreats with proof of hero fracture.

figures.name: The Architect
figures.visualHook: See `theArchitect.visualHook`. Presence fills rooms without shouting.
figures.roleInPlot: Episodes 9–12: reveal (9), psychological siege (10), parallel to ensemble recovery (11), finale confrontation (12).

supportingFigures.name: Lattice Analyst 'Grey'
supportingFigures.visualHook: Waveforms and clipped diction — competence without charisma.
supportingFigures.roleInPlot: Optional recurring pressure in ops scenes — can appear whenever Lattice needs a faceless brain.
supportingFigures.outcome: Survives; humanizes Lattice as an institution, not one body.

henchmenAndSquadTemplates.purpose: Episodes 6+ need repeatable mooks with rules so fights stay authored — not random armor guys.

latticeGrunt.label: Lattice Grunt (Uniformed operative)
latticeGrunt.visualHook: Matte armor, soft joints — built for crowd control, not duels. Face obscured; numbers on shoulder for storytelling clarity (who falls, who talks).
latticeGrunt.behavior: Follows radio doctrine; breaks when leadership breaks. Not brave — trained.

latticeElite.label: Lattice Elite (Breacher)
latticeElite.visualHook: Heavier plates, distinct silhouette — reads as 'mini-boss' in groups of 2–3 max.
latticeElite.behavior: Authorized for capture missions; tries to split the team spatially.

rulesForMooks: Mooks talk in procedure — callouts, clearance codes — never monologue.
If a mook removes a helmet, give them one human line max — then consequence — so violence has weight.
Lattice does not infinite-spawn; show logistics (vans, timing) so Nova's wins feel earned.

continuityChecks.episode5: Echo must read as **controlled** — Solace's observation must be fair on rewatch.
continuityChecks.episode9: Architect motivation must match `Season 1/season.json` villain block verbatim in spirit.
continuityChecks.episode12: Architect defeated but not destroyed — no accidental death fakeout unless scripted with purpose.
